title: Casual employees

meta.description: Definition, entitlements, and rules for casual employees under Australian law including 2024 changes.

headings.text: Definition of a casual employee (from 26 August 2024)

content:
A new definition of casual employee was introduced into the Fair Work Act from 26 August 2024 as part of the Closing Loopholes reforms.

Definition (from 26 August 2024):
An employee is a casual employee if, at the time they are offered a job, the employment relationship has no firm advance commitment to continuing and indefinite work. Key indicators include:
- No guaranteed hours of work
- Work offered and accepted on an ad-hoc basis
- No firm commitment to ongoing engagement

Casual loading:
Casual employees are entitled to a casual loading or a specific casual pay rate under their award, registered agreement, or employment contract. Generally, casual loading is an extra 25% of the minimum hourly rate. This loading compensates casual employees for the entitlements they don't receive (e.g. paid annual leave, paid personal/carer's leave).

Example: If the base rate is $24.95/hr, a casual employee would receive approximately $31.19/hr (base + 25%).

Entitlements casual employees DO receive:
- Casual loading (or specific casual pay rate)
- 2 days unpaid carer's leave per occasion
- 2 days compassionate leave per occasion
- Family and domestic violence leave (10 days paid from 1 August 2023)
- Community service leave
- Long service leave (after qualifying period)
- Public holidays (if they would ordinarily work on that day)
- Superannuation contributions from employer
- Fair Work Information Statement
- Casual Employment Information Statement (CEIS)

Entitlements casual employees do NOT receive:
- Paid annual leave
- Paid personal/carer's leave (sick leave)
- Notice of termination (unless award/agreement provides otherwise)
- Redundancy pay (generally)

Casual Employment Information Statement (CEIS):
Employers must give all new casual employees a copy of the CEIS before, or as soon as practicable after, they start employment. The CEIS explains their conditions of employment.

Pathway to permanent employment (Employee Choice Pathway):
From 26 February 2025, eligible casual employees can notify their employer in writing of their intention to change to permanent (full-time or part-time) employment. Eligibility requires:
- 6 months of employment (12 months for small business employees)
- The employment relationship is consistent with permanent employment characteristics

Employers can only refuse on specified grounds.

A casual employee can also change to permanent employment at any time if both the employer and employee agree.

Transitional arrangements:
A casual employee engaged before 26 August 2024 continues as a casual under the new rules unless they move to permanent employment through agreement or the employee choice pathway.
tables: Unpaid carer's leave (2 days per occasion)
Family and domestic violence leave (10 days paid)

key_facts.legislation: Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth), as amended by Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes) Acts 2023–2024
